content.text: Every time a neural network makes a prediction, something must measure how wrong it is—and produce a single number that gradient descent can minimize. That measuring device is the

. Picking the right loss for the task, understanding its mathematical properties, and knowing how it shapes the optimization landscape are fundamental skills for anyone training or fine-tuning models—from small classifiers to large language models.
) is a mapping from model predictions and ground-truth targets to a non-negative scalar. Formally, given a prediction
. A loss of zero means perfect prediction; higher values indicate worse predictions. Training a neural network is the process of finding weights
that minimize the expected loss over a dataset.
(or at least sub-differentiable) so that backpropagation can compute gradients with respect to every parameter. The gradient

tells each weight which direction to move to reduce the loss. The choice of loss function is not a minor implementation detail—it defines what the model is actually learning to optimize. A regression model trained with mean squared error learns to minimize average squared deviations, which implicitly assumes Gaussian noise. A language model trained with cross-entropy learns to predict the probability distribution over the next token, which underpins everything from GPT-style pretraining to instruction fine-tuning.

Loss functions sit at the intersection of statistics, information theory, and optimization. Mean squared error corresponds to maximum likelihood estimation under a Gaussian noise model. Cross-entropy corresponds to maximum likelihood estimation under a categorical distribution. Understanding these connections helps practitioners choose losses that match the probabilistic assumptions of their problem.

The loss function is the only signal that guides weight updates during training. No matter how sophisticated the architecture or optimizer, gradients flow back from the loss—change the loss and you change what the model becomes. This makes the loss the single most consequential design decision after the model architecture itself.
For large language models, the choice is nearly universal:

averaged over billions of tokens. This simple objective produces models capable of reasoning, code generation, and multilingual translation as emergent capabilities. Pretraining loss curves (and their exponentiated form,

) are the primary diagnostic: a smoothly decreasing loss indicates healthy training; spikes suggest numerical instability, bad batches, or learning rate issues. In fine-tuning stages—supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), and direct preference optimization (DPO)—the loss is redefined to encode additional objectives such as helpfulness and harmlessness. Understanding how each loss shapes gradients is essential for diagnosing training failures and designing multi-objective pipelines.

Beyond LLMs, losses drive every supervised learning task: MSE for regression, cross-entropy for classification, contrastive loss for embedding models, KL divergence for variational autoencoders and distillation, and reward modeling loss for preference learning. Mismatching the loss to the task is one of the most common causes of models that train smoothly but perform poorly at inference time.
Choosing a well-matched loss function delivers concrete improvements across the entire training pipeline:
Because the loss is a smooth function of model outputs, automatic differentiation can compute exact gradients, enabling efficient first-order optimization via SGD, Adam, and their variants.
A loss that matches the task's statistical assumptions (e.g., cross-entropy for discrete distributions) causes the model to converge to the maximum likelihood solution for that task.
Many losses have natural extensions—label smoothing, focal weighting, class reweighting—that improve generalization without changing the model architecture.
Loss curves, perplexity, and bits-per-character are intuitive quantities that reveal training progress, overfitting, and data quality issues at a glance.
Multiple loss terms can be combined (e.g., task loss + KL penalty in RLHF PPO) to encode complex objectives, with weights controlling the trade-off between competing goals.
Standard losses like cross-entropy are fused into highly optimized GPU kernels in frameworks like PyTorch, making the loss computation a negligible fraction of total training time even at scale.
Every loss function embeds assumptions that may not hold for every problem. Understanding these limitations prevents silent failure modes:

Squaring errors gives extreme values quadratically more influence. A single outlier can dominate the gradient and destabilize training. Huber loss or MAE are more robust alternatives for noisy regression.

The loss is minimized by assigning all probability mass to the correct class, which encourages models to be maximally confident. On out-of-distribution inputs, this produces poorly calibrated predictions. Label smoothing partially mitigates this.
If 99% of samples belong to class 0, the model can achieve low average cross-entropy by always predicting class 0. Weighted cross-entropy or focal loss rebalances the contribution of rare classes.
Next-token loss and downstream capability diverge.

A lower pretraining perplexity does not guarantee better performance on specific downstream tasks. Evaluation benchmarks measure different things than the training objective, which is why fine-tuning and RLHF exist.

The metrics practitioners care about—BLEU, accuracy, human preference—are often non-differentiable. The loss is a proxy; optimizing the proxy too aggressively can diverge from the true goal (Goodhart's Law in ML).

Computing log(softmax(logits)) naively can overflow or underflow in float16. Framework implementations fuse softmax and log into a numerically stable log-sum-exp form—using raw predictions with custom log operations is risky.
During a forward pass, the model transforms input
through layers of weights to produce raw output logits
. The loss function receives these outputs alongside the ground-truth target and returns a scalar. Backpropagation then computes
and propagates this gradient through the computation graph to every parameter in the network.
MSE is the workhorse loss for regression tasks. For a batch of
The gradient with respect to prediction
. This means weights that produced a large error receive a large gradient update. MSE corresponds to maximum likelihood estimation under the assumption that residuals
Cross-entropy measures the information cost of encoding samples from the true distribution using the model's predicted distribution. For a categorical prediction over
In practice, the model outputs raw logits
and the loss is computed as log-softmax plus negative log-likelihood in a single numerically stable operation:

—the predicted probability minus the one-hot target. This gradient is large when the model assigns low probability to the correct class and small when it assigns high probability, creating a natural learning signal.
KL divergence measures how one probability distribution
(the model) differs from a reference distribution
KL divergence equals cross-entropy minus entropy:

is fixed (e.g., the teacher model's output distribution in knowledge distillation), minimizing KL divergence is equivalent to minimizing cross-entropy between the student and teacher. KL divergence is also the penalty term in PPO-based RLHF to prevent the policy from straying too far from the reference model.
Perplexity is the exponentiated average per-token cross-entropy, commonly used to evaluate language models on held-out text:

A perplexity of 10 means the model is as uncertain as if it were choosing uniformly among 10 equally likely tokens at each position. Lower is better. State-of-the-art LLMs achieve single-digit perplexity on standard benchmarks like Penn Treebank and WikiText-103.
Label smoothing replaces the hard one-hot target

is a small constant (typically 0.1). This prevents the model from becoming overconfident by never assigning probability zero to incorrect classes during training. The cross-entropy loss under label smoothing becomes:

The diagram below shows where the loss function sits in a complete training iteration. The forward pass flows left to right: input data through the network to predictions, then into the loss function with the ground-truth labels to produce a scalar. The backward pass flows right to left: the scalar loss triggers backpropagation, which computes parameter gradients that the optimizer uses to update weights.

attrs.alt: Training loop diagram showing the role of the loss function in forward and backward passes

content.text: Three loss families are shown in the lower panel, corresponding to the three most common scenarios in agentic AI: MSE for continuous prediction heads, cross-entropy for classification and next-token prediction, and KL divergence for distribution matching in distillation and RLHF. Each produces a different gradient landscape, which influences convergence speed and the type of solution the network finds.
Selecting the appropriate loss is a function of the output type, the noise model, and the downstream evaluation metric:
Use for regression tasks where the output is a continuous scalar or vector (price prediction, coordinate regression, embedding similarity). Prefer Huber loss when the dataset contains outliers.
Use when the output is a probability distribution over a discrete set of classes (image classification, intent detection, next-token prediction in LLMs). This is the default for language modeling.
Use for binary outputs or multi-label classification where each class is independent (spam detection, multi-label tagging, safety classifiers).
Use when the learning target is a full probability distribution rather than a single class—knowledge distillation, VAE latent regularization, RLHF KL penalty, and soft-label training.
Use for metric learning and embedding alignment (sentence embeddings, retrieval models, CLIP-style vision-language training).
Use when training a preference model from pairwise comparisons (RLHF stage 2). The Bradley-Terry model treats preference as logistic regression over reward differences.
When not to use a single scalar loss directly:

When the evaluation metric is non-differentiable (e.g., BLEU, exact match, human win rate), the loss is a surrogate. In that case, use the loss for gradient-based training but evaluate on the true metric with held-out data, and consider reinforcement learning or DPO to directly optimize the desired signal.

The following example shows a complete training step with proper loss computation in PyTorch, covering the most common pitfalls: passing logits (not softmax outputs) to the loss, masking padding positions, and computing perplexity from the loss value.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

# ── Regression: MSE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def mse_step(model, optimizer, x, y):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    pred = model(x)                         # shape: (batch,)
    loss = F.mse_loss(pred, y)              # mean over batch
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()

# ── Classification: cross-entropy ───────────────────────────────────────
def ce_step(model, optimizer, x, labels):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(x)                       # shape: (batch, num_classes)
    # IMPORTANT: pass logits, NOT softmax(logits)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels, label_smoothing=0.1)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()

# ── Language modeling: token-level cross-entropy with padding mask ───────
def lm_step(model, optimizer, input_ids, labels, vocab_size, pad_id=-100):
    """
    input_ids : (batch, seq_len)
    labels    : (batch, seq_len)  — shift of input_ids; pad positions = -100
    """
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(input_ids)               # (batch, seq_len, vocab_size)

    # Flatten for cross_entropy: (batch*seq_len, vocab_size), (batch*seq_len,)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(
        logits.view(-1, vocab_size),
        labels.view(-1),
        ignore_index=pad_id,               # skip PAD positions
    )
    perplexity = math.exp(loss.item())

    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item(), perplexity

# ── KL divergence: distillation ─────────────────────────────────────────
def distillation_step(student, teacher, optimizer, x, labels,
                      temp=4.0, alpha=0.5):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    s_logits = student(x)
    with torch.no_grad():
        t_logits = teacher(x)

    # Soft targets from teacher (temperature scaling)
    t_probs = F.softmax(t_logits / temp, dim=-1)
    s_log_probs = F.log_softmax(s_logits / temp, dim=-1)
    kl_loss = F.kl_div(s_log_probs, t_probs, reduction="batchmean") * (temp ** 2)

    # Hard labels from ground truth
    ce_loss = F.cross_entropy(s_logits, labels)

    # Combined objective
    loss = alpha * kl_loss + (1 - alpha) * ce_loss
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()
expects raw logits and applies log-softmax internally using the numerically stable log-sum-exp trick. Setting

tells PyTorch to skip positions where the label equals -100, which is the convention used by Hugging Face Transformers for padding. The temperature parameter in distillation softens the teacher's distribution; squaring it in the loss weight compensates for the scale change caused by dividing logits by temperature.

The first example verifies MSE and cross-entropy numerically on a minimal tensor, making the math concrete. The second example trains a small feed-forward classifier from scratch to show the full loop.

# Example 1 — Manual verification of loss values
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

# MSE: two samples
pred   = torch.tensor([2.5, 0.0])
target = torch.tensor([3.0, 1.0])
mse = F.mse_loss(pred, target)
# Expected: ((2.5-3.0)^2 + (0.0-1.0)^2) / 2 = (0.25 + 1.0) / 2 = 0.625
print(f"MSE = {mse.item():.4f}")          # 0.6250

# Cross-entropy: one sample, 3 classes, true class = 0
logits = torch.tensor([[2.0, 1.0, 0.5]])  # raw scores (logits)
label  = torch.tensor([0])
ce = F.cross_entropy(logits, label)
# Manual: log-sum-exp = log(e^2 + e^1 + e^0.5) ≈ 2.4076
#         CE = 2.4076 - 2.0 = 0.4076
print(f"CE  = {ce.item():.4f}")           # ~0.4076

# Perplexity from per-token loss
per_token_loss = torch.tensor([1.2, 0.9, 1.5, 0.7])
avg_loss = per_token_loss.mean()
ppl = math.exp(avg_loss.item())
print(f"PPL = {ppl:.2f}")                 # e^1.075 ≈ 2.93

# Label smoothing: compare CE with and without
logits2 = torch.tensor([[3.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.2]])
label2  = torch.tensor([0])
ce_hard = F.cross_entropy(logits2, label2, label_smoothing=0.0)
ce_smooth = F.cross_entropy(logits2, label2, label_smoothing=0.1)
print(f"CE (hard)   = {ce_hard.item():.4f}")    # lower, overconfident
print(f"CE (smooth) = {ce_smooth.item():.4f}")  # slightly higher
The second example trains a complete binary classifier on synthetic data with binary cross-entropy, showing validation loss tracking:

# Example 2 — Training a classifier from scratch
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

torch.manual_seed(42)

# Synthetic 2D data: two Gaussian clusters
def make_data(n=500):
    pos = torch.randn(n, 2) + torch.tensor([2.0, 2.0])
    neg = torch.randn(n, 2) + torch.tensor([-2.0, -2.0])
    X = torch.cat([pos, neg])
    y = torch.cat([torch.ones(n), torch.zeros(n)]).unsqueeze(1)
    return X, y

X_train, y_train = make_data(400)
X_val,   y_val   = make_data(100)

# Model: 2 → 16 → 1 with sigmoid output for BCE
model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(2, 16),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(16, 1),
)
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

for epoch in range(50):
    # Training step
    model.train()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(X_train)                            # (800, 1)
    # BCEWithLogitsLoss: numerically stable, expects logits
    train_loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(logits, y_train)
    train_loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

    # Validation
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        val_logits = model(X_val)
        val_loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(val_logits, y_val)
        preds = (val_logits.sigmoid() > 0.5).float()
        acc = (preds == y_val).float().mean()

    if epoch % 10 == 0:
        print(f"Epoch {epoch:3d} | train={train_loss:.4f} | "
              f"val={val_loss:.4f} | acc={acc:.3f}")

# Output (approx):
# Epoch   0 | train=0.6748 | val=0.6512 | acc=0.730
# Epoch  10 | train=0.1503 | val=0.1440 | acc=0.960
# Epoch  40 | train=0.0621 | val=0.0592 | acc=0.990
The following code mirrors how Hugging Face's

computes language modeling loss during causal LM fine-tuning. The labels tensor is the input shifted by one position, with padding tokens replaced by -100. This is the exact pattern used in GPT-2, LLaMA, and Mistral fine-tuning runs.

# Causal LM loss — the pattern used in GPT-style fine-tuning
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

model_name = "gpt2"  # replace with any causal LM
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model      = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

text = [
    "The transformer architecture revolutionized NLP.",
    "Attention mechanisms allow models to weigh token importance.",
]

enc = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", padding=True, truncation=True,
                max_length=32)
input_ids      = enc["input_ids"]       # (2, 32)
attention_mask = enc["attention_mask"]  # (2, 32)

# Labels: same as input_ids, but pad positions → -100 (ignored by loss)
labels = input_ids.clone()
labels[attention_mask == 0] = -100

# Shift inside the model: logits[i] predicts token i+1
with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(input_ids=input_ids,
                    attention_mask=attention_mask,
                    labels=labels)

loss = outputs.loss                      # averaged over non-padded tokens
perplexity = math.exp(loss.item())
print(f"NLL loss  : {loss.item():.4f}")  # ~3.1 on random text for gpt2
print(f"Perplexity: {perplexity:.2f}")

# Manual equivalent of what the model computes internally:
logits = outputs.logits                  # (2, 32, vocab_size)
shift_logits = logits[:, :-1, :].contiguous()
shift_labels = labels[:, 1:].contiguous()
manual_loss = F.cross_entropy(
    shift_logits.view(-1, logits.size(-1)),
    shift_labels.view(-1),
    ignore_index=-100,
)
print(f"Manual match: {manual_loss.item():.4f}")  # identical to outputs.loss
The shift by one position is the key operation: at each sequence position the model predicts the next token, so logits at position
are compared against the label at position
. Positions corresponding to padding are excluded via
, ensuring that the loss reflects only the model's uncertainty about real tokens, not its forced predictions over padding.
apply log-softmax internally. Passing softmax-ed probabilities produces double-softmax, destroying the gradient signal and inflating the loss.

MSE treats class indices as real numbers (distance from 0 to 2 = distance from 1 to 3), which has no semantic meaning for categories. The result is slow convergence and poor calibration. Always use cross-entropy for discrete outputs.
The target must be a LongTensor of class indices, not a FloatTensor of one-hot vectors. Passing float targets raises a RuntimeError or silently computes nonsense (use
Forgetting to mask padding in sequence models.

, padding positions contribute an artificial loss signal (the model sees a target token at a padding position and tries to predict it). This can significantly distort gradients in batches with variable-length sequences.

Training loss always decreases if the optimizer is working. The diagnostic signal is the gap between training and validation loss. A widening gap indicates overfitting; flat validation loss with decreasing training loss means the model has memorized the training set.
introduces floating-point errors, especially in float16 mixed precision training. Always use the fused log-softmax.

# WRONG: double softmax
probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
loss = F.cross_entropy(probs, labels)        # loss is meaningless

# WRONG: non-stable log
loss = -torch.log(torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1))[range(B), labels].mean()

# RIGHT: pass logits directly — PyTorch handles everything internally
loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels)

# RIGHT (stable manual version using log_softmax):
log_probs = F.log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)
loss = F.nll_loss(log_probs, labels)
. Never pre-apply softmax or sigmoid—the fused implementations are faster and numerically stable.
when computing language model or sequence-to-sequence loss over padded batches. Fill the labels tensor with -100 at all padding and prompt positions.
Apply label smoothing (0.05–0.1) when fine-tuning classifiers on small datasets or when the model shows signs of overconfidence at training time.
Track perplexity alongside raw NLL during language model training. Perplexity is more interpretable and makes it easy to spot anomalous spikes that signal instability.
and weight samples manually when implementing focal loss or class-weighted cross-entropy, rather than modifying loss objects in ways that alter the gradient scale.

In multi-task training, normalize individual loss terms before summing. Unnormalized losses from tasks with different scales will cause one task's gradient to dominate regardless of the loss weights you set.

Validate loss magnitudes at initialization. For cross-entropy with C classes and random weights, the initial loss should be approximately log(C). If it is much higher or lower, check for softmax issues, initialization problems, or data leaks.

Loss computation is typically a small fraction of total training time for large models—the forward pass through transformer layers dominates. However, at vocabulary sizes of 100K+ tokens, the final linear layer and cross-entropy over the full vocabulary can become a bottleneck.

The weight matrix for the output projection is shape (hidden_dim, vocab_size). For a 4096-dim model with 128K vocabulary, this is 4096 × 128000 ≈ 524M parameters—over half a billion just for the output layer. Some architectures tie the embedding and projection weights to save memory.
Computing softmax over a 128K-token vocabulary for a full sequence in one shot requires materializing a large float tensor (batch × seq_len × vocab_size). Libraries like
implement chunked or fused cross-entropy that avoids materializing the full probability tensor, reducing memory by up to 4×.
Loss computation runs in float32 even when the rest of the model uses bf16, because log-softmax requires high precision. PyTorch's autocast context handles this automatically via
When batch size is constrained by GPU memory, accumulate gradients over multiple micro-batches before calling
on each micro-batch so the effective learning rate stays consistent.
In data-parallel training, each GPU computes loss over its shard. Frameworks like DeepSpeed and PyTorch FSDP aggregate the loss with
and divide by world size to get the global mean. This must happen before logging but after
Loss functions are implemented across every major deep learning framework, with high-performance variants available for specialized use cases:

# ── PyTorch core losses ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
import torch.nn as nn

nn.MSELoss()                       # regression
nn.L1Loss()                        # MAE, robust to outliers
nn.HuberLoss(delta=1.0)            # MSE for small errors, MAE for large
nn.CrossEntropyLoss(
    weight=class_weights,          # rebalance imbalanced classes
    label_smoothing=0.1,
    ignore_index=-100,
)
nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()             # binary / multi-label
nn.KLDivLoss(reduction="batchmean")# distillation, VAE

# ── Hugging Face — built-in LM loss ─────────────────────────────────────
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")
outputs = model(input_ids=input_ids, labels=labels)
loss = outputs.loss               # cross-entropy, shift handled internally

# ── Liger kernel — memory-efficient fused cross-entropy ─────────────────
# pip install liger-kernel
from liger_kernel.transformers import apply_liger_kernel_to_llama
apply_liger_kernel_to_llama()     # patch LLaMA models with fused kernels
# After patching, standard training code uses 30–40% less GPU memory

# ── torchmetrics — validation metrics alongside loss ────────────────────
# pip install torchmetrics
from torchmetrics import Accuracy, Perplexity

acc_metric = Accuracy(task="multiclass", num_classes=10)
ppl_metric = Perplexity(ignore_index=-100)

# ── TensorFlow / Keras ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
import tensorflow as tf

model.compile(
    loss=tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy(from_logits=True),
    optimizer="adam",
)

library provides ready-made trainers for SFT (cross-entropy on completions only), reward modeling (Bradley-Terry pairwise loss), PPO (policy gradient with KL penalty), and DPO (direct preference optimization). These abstract away the multi-component loss bookkeeping while remaining configurable.
The following table compares the most common loss functions by task, properties, and typical use cases in the LLM and agentic AI ecosystem:
Quadratic for small errors, linear for large
Loss functions are not typically a security attack surface, but incorrect loss design creates serious reliability risks in production ML systems:
A single batch containing all tokens from one class, or a batch where the model assigns near-zero probability to every correct token, can produce a loss value of
. This corrupts model weights and crashes training. Guard against this with gradient clipping (
), loss value monitoring, and mixed-precision loss scaling.

In RLHF, the reward model is a proxy for human preference. If the PPO agent optimizes the reward too aggressively (low KL penalty), it can find degenerate outputs that score well on the learned reward but poorly by actual human evaluation. Maintain the KL penalty and re-run human evaluation periodically.

Backdoor attacks inject poisoned samples designed to produce high loss on clean inputs and low loss on trigger inputs. The gradient from poisoned batches can steer the model toward attacker-controlled behavior. Robust training methods (e.g., outlier loss filtering, data curation) mitigate this.

In federated learning, the loss value itself can leak information about private training data through gradient inversion attacks. Differential privacy techniques add calibrated noise to gradients before sharing them with a central server.
Overfitting to the loss without monitoring eval metrics.

A model can achieve near-zero training loss while completely failing on held-out data if the training set is small or the model is overparameterized. Always maintain a validation split and track evaluation metrics separately from the training loss.

Think of the loss function as a GPS elevation map, and the model's weights as a hiker's position on that map. Each weight configuration corresponds to a point in a high-dimensional space, and the loss function assigns an altitude to every point—lower altitude means better predictions. Gradient descent is the hiker moving downhill: at each step, the hiker senses the slope in every direction (the gradient), then takes a step proportional to the learning rate in the direction of steepest descent.

Cross-entropy, in information-theoretic terms, measures bits of surprise. If the model assigns 50% probability to the correct next token, the loss is 1 bit. If it assigns 1%, the loss is about 6.6 bits—the model is very surprised. Training nudges every weight to be less surprised, on average, by the training corpus. After sufficient training on enough diverse data, the model has internalized statistical regularities of language sufficiently well that it is rarely more than mildly surprised—and that capacity to predict tokens accurately turns out to be the same capacity that enables reasoning, translation, and code generation.
A loss function converts model predictions and ground-truth targets into a single differentiable scalar that gradient descent minimizes—it is the primary signal driving every weight update.

Cross-entropy is the default for classification and language modeling; it corresponds to maximum likelihood under a categorical distribution and is what LLMs minimize during pretraining over next-token prediction.
MSE assumes Gaussian residuals and is correct for regression; use Huber loss when the dataset contains outliers, since squaring large errors gives them disproportionate influence on gradients.

Perplexity is the exponentiated average per-token cross-entropy loss; lower perplexity means the model assigns higher probability to held-out text, and it is the standard metric for comparing language models.

Always pass raw logits to PyTorch's cross-entropy functions—they apply log-softmax internally using a numerically stable log-sum-exp algorithm; pre-applying softmax causes double-softmax and corrupts the loss.
on sequence-level losses to exclude padding and prompt tokens from the gradient; without masking, the model wastes capacity trying to predict meaningless padding targets.

Label smoothing (ε≈0.1) and KL-divergence penalties prevent models from becoming overconfident: smoothing softens training targets, while KL penalties in RLHF keep the policy close to a reference model.

The training loss is an optimization proxy; always track held-out metrics alongside it—a model can reach near-zero training loss while failing badly on evaluation tasks due to overfitting or misaligned objectives.
